narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 51.9% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings, both at the 1st decile—meaning you were in the bottom 10% of your peers—compared to typical students who average around the 5th or 6th decile. Your LSAT score of 28 is also notably lower than the average of about 37, and your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 sits just below the usual 3.21. Other details like your lower-middle family income bracket, Asian racial background, male gender, and plan to attend full-time played smaller roles, as these align somewhat with common profiles—full-time is the norm for over 90% of students, males make up about 56%, and Asian students are around 6% of applicants. In assessing your case, the model weighed your Year 3 decile ranking as the single biggest factor pushing toward a failure prediction, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, then your LSAT score, family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status—all of which nudged the outcome slightly toward higher risk rather than a strong pass. These low early academic deciles stood out the most because they signal challenges in keeping up with coursework compared to peers who typically rank higher. Your LSAT being well below average amplified concerns about analytical skills needed for the bar, while your GPA hinted at similar hurdles. To improve your odds for future applications or bar success, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above 37, strengthen your GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program if possible, and build a track record of higher decile performance in any further studies. Family background and demographics aren't changeable, but excelling academically can outweigh them. Many students in tough spots have turned things around by targeting these areas—consider bar prep resources early or seeking mentorship from successful alumni. With targeted effort, you could shift that prediction firmly toward passing and open doors to admission next time. Keep pushing; your full-time commitment is already a positive step most applicants share.
